Iteration 3 contains one run per eval/config pair, so the new discrimination claims are about prompt and expectation sharpness, not stability across repeated samples.

The benchmark now captures skill value better than iteration 2: with-skill now beats baseline on 7 of 8 evals (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8), ties only on eval 5, and never loses. The aggregate delta (`+0.16` pass rate) now matches a broad per-eval advantage instead of being propped up by a few isolated wins.

Revised eval 3 became more discriminating. In iteration 2 it was a full tie at 100% for both sides; in iteration 3 the with-skill run passes 7/7 while without-skill drops to 6/7 by missing the new same-task/contextvars leakage assertion. That change is useful because it separates generic pytest/AnyIO advice from the more specific runner semantics the skill is supposed to enforce.

Revised eval 8 also became more discriminating. In iteration 2 both sides tied at 4/5 and missed the `RuntimeError` readiness-failure detail; in iteration 3 with-skill passes 6/6 while without-skill misses the stronger framing that `sleep(0.1)` is hiding a startup-handshake bug. That is a better test of structured-concurrency reasoning than the older near-tie.

Revised eval 5 did not become more discriminating. It expanded from 6 assertions to 7, but both configurations still pass 7/7, so it remains saturated and mostly measures general model competence on task ownership/lifetime rather than incremental skill value.

The only full eval-level tie left is eval 5, but there are still assertion-level ties on misses elsewhere. Eval 6 still has a shared miss on the exact `from_thread.run()` / `from_thread.run_sync()` worker-thread scope claim, which means that area is still weakly captured even though the eval overall discriminates.

The revised discriminators are mostly about explicitness and boundary language, not a different overall solution family. Baseline answers often sound broadly right, but the skill is what makes the model state the sharper claims about same-task leakage, startup-handshake bugs, cancellation ownership, runtime gating, and concrete verification steps.

That is evidence the benchmark now measures the intended skill value more honestly: not 'can the model talk about async at all', but 'does it make the exact portability/lifecycle distinctions this skill encodes'. Iteration 2 still had three ties on evals 3, 5, and 8; iteration 3 removes two of those three ties.

Cost signals remain secondary. With-skill time is basically flat overall (`+0.6s` mean), and tokens are also nearly flat (`+96`), so the sharper discrimination in evals 3 and 8 is not being bought with a large systematic resource penalty.
